criteria: The agent has read Section 3.1 completely to understand the VAE fine-tuning stage with the scale-consistency loss and its four components: reconstruction, scale consistency, KL regularization, and perceptual (LPIPS) loss.
The agent has read Section 3.2 completely to understand how wavelet-derived frequency saliency maps are computed from high-frequency DWT subbands (LH, HL, HH).
The agent has read Section 3.3 completely to understand the adaptive flow-matching objective, the time-dependent binary mask, and the final masked loss formulation.
The agent has read Section 4.1 completely to understand the experimental setup: datasets (Aesthetic-4K and LAION-High-Res), implementation with PyTorch and pytorch-wavelets, and evaluation metrics.

The agent has read Appendix A.2 and D to understand the key hyperparameters: lower bound l=0.3, VAE loss weights alpha=0.25, beta=0.001, lambda=0.05, 60K VAE fine-tuning steps, batch size 4, and learning rate 1e-5.
The agent has read Tables 1 and 2 to understand the main quantitative results on 2K and 4K image generation across multiple backbone models and datasets.
The agent has read Table 3 to understand the VAE reconstruction ablation results with and without Scale-Consistency (SC) loss, across SD3-VAE, Flux-VAE, and Sana-AE architectures.
The agent has read Table 4 to understand the component ablation study evaluating the contributions of VAE scale-consistency and wavelet masking on Diffusion4k backbone.
The plan includes both sequential training stages: (1) VAE fine-tuning with scale-consistency loss and (2) diffusion model fine-tuning with wavelet-masked flow-matching loss.

The plan includes the four-term VAE loss (Eq. 1): pixel reconstruction, scale-consistency via downsampled inputs, KL divergence regularization, and LPIPS perceptual loss, with weights alpha, beta, lambda.
The plan includes computing the DWT-based wavelet saliency map A_wavelet from the latent tensor using the Haar wavelet, aggregating high-frequency energy from LH, HL, and HH subbands.
The plan includes the time-dependent binary mask M_t construction using the wavelet saliency map and lower bound l, ensuring all regions receive at least l*T steps of supervision.
The plan includes the datasets to be used: Aesthetic-4K for 4K evaluation and LAION-High-Res (50K 2K + 20K 4K image-caption pairs) for training.
The plan includes evaluation on multiple backbone models: URAE (Flux), SD3-F16 (Diffusion-4K), PixArt-Sigma-XL, and Sana-1.6B.
The plan includes evaluation metrics: FID, LPIPS, CLIPScore, Aesthetics score, GLCM Score, and JPEG Compression Ratio.

The plan includes ablation studies: (1) VAE with/without SC loss (Table 3), (2) component contributions (Table 4), (3) wavelet basis comparison Haar/Daubechies/FFT (Table 5), and (4) masking lower bound l sweep (Table 6).

The VAE fine-tuning loss is implemented as Equation 1 with all four terms: pixel-space reconstruction loss ||D(z) - x||^2, scale-consistency loss ||D(E(z_down)) - x_down||^2, KL divergence D_KL(q(z|x)||p(z)), and LPIPS perceptual loss.
The VAE loss weights are set correctly: alpha=0.25 for scale-consistency, beta=0.001 for KL regularization, and lambda=0.05 for LPIPS perceptual loss.
The scale-consistency term correctly encodes a downsampled version of the image x_down through the encoder and decoder to compute the multi-resolution reconstruction loss.
The single-level Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) is applied to the latent tensor z in R^{C x H x W} using the Haar wavelet (via pytorch-wavelets), producing four subbands {z_LL, z_LH, z_HL, z_HH}.

The high-frequency energy map E(i,j) is computed as the mean across channels of the sum of squared LH, HL, and HH subband coefficients, as specified in Equation 3: E(i,j) = (1/C) * sum_c [(z_LH^{c,i,j})^2 + (z_HL^{c,i,j})^2 + (z_HH^{c,i,j})^2].
The energy map E (of size H/2 x W/2) is bilinearly upsampled to H x W and then min-max normalized per sample to obtain A_wavelet in [0,1]^{H x W}.
The flow-matching interpolation is implemented as z_t = (1-t)*z_0 + t*epsilon with epsilon ~ N(0, I), as specified in Equation 4.
The baseline flow-matching loss L_fm is implemented as the squared L2 norm between the target velocity (epsilon - z_0) and the predicted velocity v_theta(z_t, t, y), as specified in Equation 5.
The time-dependent binary mask M_t(i,j) is implemented as in Equation 6: M_t(i,j) = 1 if T*(A_wavelet(i,j) + l) >= t, else 0, where T is total timesteps and l is the lower bound.
The lower bound parameter l is set to 0.3, ensuring each spatial location receives at least 30% of supervision steps.

The final masked flow-matching loss L_masked is implemented as the squared L2 norm of M_t element-wise multiplied with the prediction error [(epsilon - z_0) - v_theta(z_t, t, y)], as specified in Equation 7.
The LWD framework is implemented to be model-agnostic (plug-and-play), requiring no architectural modifications to the underlying diffusion model, adding zero inference cost.

The evaluation metrics are implemented: FID, LPIPS, CLIPScore, Aesthetics score (using LAION aesthetic predictor), GLCM Score (Gray-Level Co-occurrence Matrix for texture), and JPEG Compression Ratio.
The perceptual quality metrics are implemented: MAN-IQA, HPSv2.1 (Human Preference Score), and PickScore for evaluating generated image quality.

The frequency-aware evaluation metrics are implemented in Appendix C: HLFR (High/Low Frequency Ratio), RDR (Ratio Difference from Real), WQS (Wavelet Quality Score), HFE (High-Frequency Energy), and HFEI (High-Frequency Emphasis Index).
The VAE fine-tuning is implemented with the ablation comparison between standard VAE and SC-regularized VAE across architectures: SD3-VAE, Flux-VAE, and Sana-AE.
The wavelet basis ablation is implemented comparing Haar wavelet, Daubechies (db2) wavelet, and FFT High-Pass filtering for the saliency map computation on the Diffusion4k backbone.
The masking lower bound ablation is implemented by varying l in {0.0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7} and evaluating FID, GLCM Score, and CLIPScore.
The VAE fine-tuning is executed for 60K steps with batch size 4 and learning rate 1e-5 on NVIDIA A100 GPUs.
The LWD training on the Aesthetic-4K and LAION-High-Res datasets is executed for each backbone (URAE/Flux, SD3-Diff4k, PixArt-Sigma-XL, Sana-1.6B) with the configurations specified in Table 9.
The 2K image generation experiment using HPD benchmark prompts is executed for all baselines and LWD variants, producing outputs for Table 1 evaluation.
The 2K and 4K image generation experiment using Aesthetic-Eval prompts is executed for all backbone models and LWD variants, producing outputs for Table 2 evaluation.
The 4K image generation experiment using HPD benchmark prompts is executed for PixArt-Sigma-XL, Sana-1.6B, FLUX-1.dev, URAE, and LWD+URAE, producing outputs for Table 7 evaluation.
The VAE reconstruction ablation is executed evaluating SD3-VAE, SD3-VAE-F16, SD3-VAE-F16-SC, Flux-VAE, Flux-VAE-SC, Sana-AE, and Sana-AE-SC on the Aesthetic-4K validation set.
The component contribution ablation is executed with four configurations (baseline, +VAE-SC, +wavelet masking, full LWD) on the Diffusion4k backbone at 2048x2048 resolution on the Aesthetic dataset.
The wavelet basis ablation is executed comparing Haar, Daubechies, and FFT High-Pass methods on the Diffusion4k backbone at 2048x2048 on the Aesthetic dataset.
The masking lower bound ablation is executed for l in {0.0, 0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 0.7} evaluating FID, GLCM Score, and CLIPScore.

The frequency-aware evaluation is executed on the Aesthetic-4K validation set for Sana-1.6B, LWD+Sana-1.6B, SD3-Diff4k-F16, LWD+SD3-F16, PixArt-Sigma-XL, and LWD+PixArt-Sigma-XL, reporting HLFR, RDFR, WQS, HFE, HFEI, FSIM, and MS-SSIM.
The VAE reconstruction results match Table 3: Flux-VAE-SC achieves rFID of 0.50 (vs 0.73 baseline), PSNR 28.14, SSIM 0.90, LPIPS 0.06; SD3-VAE-F16-SC achieves LPIPS of 0.18 (vs 0.30 baseline).
The 2K generation results on HPD prompts match Table 1: LWD+URAE achieves FID of 32.88 (vs URAE baseline of 35.25) and LPIPS of 0.6336 (vs 0.6717), demonstrating consistent improvement over URAE.

The 2K generation results on Aesthetic-Eval match Table 2: LWD+SD3-F16 achieves FID 38.74 (vs SD3-F16 at 43.82), LWD+PixArt-Sigma-XL achieves FID 36.14 (vs 39.13), demonstrating FID improvement across backbones.

The 4K generation results on Aesthetic-Eval match Table 2: LWD+SD3-F16 achieves CLIPScore 34.08 (vs SD3-F16 at 33.12) and Aesthetics 6.03 (vs 5.97); LWD+Sana-1.6B achieves GLCM 0.60 (vs 0.39) and Compression Ratio 32.62 (vs 48.36).

The 4K generation results on HPD prompts match Table 7: LWD+URAE achieves MAN-IQA 0.4011 (vs URAE 0.3850), QualiCLIP 0.3855 (vs 0.3758), and GLCM Score 0.74 (vs 0.41), achieving the best overall scores.

The component ablation results match Table 4: the full LWD framework achieves the best FID (38.74), CLIPScore (34.94), and Aesthetics (6.17) compared to individual components added incrementally to the SD3-Diff4k-F16 baseline.
The wavelet basis ablation results match Table 5: LWD with Haar achieves the best FID (38.74), Aesthetics (6.17), and GLCM (0.74); FFT High-Pass achieves lower cost (0.875ms) but worse FID (39.45).

The masking lower bound ablation results match Table 6: l=0.3 achieves the optimal FID (32.88) and GLCM Score (0.74); performance degrades for l=0.0, l=0.5, and l=0.7, confirming l=0.3 as the optimal hyperparameter.

The frequency-aware evaluation results match Table 8: LWD+SD3-F16 achieves HLFR 0.0555 (closest to real 0.0560), RDFR 0.0437 (best among models), and WQS 0.4735 (highest), demonstrating improved frequency alignment.
